worth_it_explanation: A publisher who sends inventory through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) is the party that knows which pages and apps are directed to children, and APS and its demand partners rely on that signal to suppress personalized ads and restrict data use on those impressions. If the signal is missing or wrong, personal information from children can flow into behavioral advertising, which is the exact harm the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) exists to prevent, and the publisher carries the regulatory exposure. The check is a review of how child-directed inventory is flagged and what data treatment follows, so it costs reading and console time rather than a rebuild, and the finding points to a concrete configuration the publisher can correct.
intro: APS is a server-side header-bidding suite, and the bid requests it sends carry regulatory signals that demand partners read before deciding how to treat an impression. One of those signals marks inventory that is directed to children, which tells the auction to suppress personalized advertising and limit the data collected. This subcategory checks whether a publisher's child-directed inventory is flagged so that personalized ads and certain data uses are suppressed, and whether restricted data processing reaches the inventory likely to be seen by minors. These checks confirm that the child-directed treatment is set deliberately on the right inventory rather than left at a default that does not match where children actually are.

audit_checks.title: Child-directed inventory is not flagged for suppressed personalization
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When inventory directed to children is not flagged, the bid requests Amazon Publisher Services sends carry no signal to suppress personalized ads, so demand partners may apply behavioral targeting and set tracking identifiers on impressions that reach children. That is a direct COPPA exposure for the publisher since personal information from a child is collected for advertising without the treatment the rule requires.

educate.base:
The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) governs how personal information may be collected from children under 13 on sites and services that are directed to children. In a programmatic auction, the way a publisher applies COPPA is to flag the impression so the rest of the supply chain knows to treat it as child-directed. The OpenRTB specification that Amazon Publisher Services (APS) and its demand partners speak carries a regulatory flag for exactly this purpose. The IAB Tech Lab OpenRTB Regs object defines a 'coppa' field as a flag indicating whether the request is subject to COPPA, where 0 means no and 1 means yes.

When that flag is set, the impression is supposed to receive child-directed treatment. Personalized and behavioral advertising is suppressed, and trackers, identity syncs, and measurement that depend on personal data are not added to the impression. When the flag is missing on inventory that is in fact directed to children, none of that suppression happens. Demand partners receive the impression as an ordinary one and may target it behaviorally and set identifiers, so personal information from a child can be collected for advertising. The publisher is the party that knows which pages and apps are directed to children, so the publisher is the party responsible for setting the signal.

Child-directed treatment is not all-or-nothing across a property. The FTC's guidance is that COPPA can apply to a portion of a general-audience service, not only to sites built entirely for kids, so a publisher may have a children's section, a kids' app, or specific pages that are directed to children inside a broader property. The flagging has to follow that reality and cover the right inventory rather than the whole account or none of it.

A few signs point to child-directed inventory that is not flagged:
  • The property, or a section of it, is directed to children, but no child-directed treatment is configured for the APS inventory that serves there
  • Personalized or behavioral demand is being served on pages or apps aimed at children, which indicates the suppression signal is not reaching the auction
  • The publisher cannot point to where the child-directed flag is set for the inventory in question
You may want to confirm that every slice of inventory directed to children carries a child-directed signal into the APS auction. One approach is to list the pages, sections, and apps that are directed to children, then check that each one is configured for child-directed treatment so personalized ads and tracking are suppressed there.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a kids' section, a games area, or family-oriented content can be directed to children even when the rest of the site is built for adults. You may want to map which sections fall under COPPA and confirm that the APS inventory on those sections is flagged for child-directed treatment, so the suppression applies to the children's content without disturbing the personalized demand on the general-audience pages.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a toys or kids' product area, or content reviewing children's products, can draw an audience of children and parents shopping for them. You may want to confirm whether those areas are directed to children under the rule, and if so, flag the APS inventory there for child-directed treatment so personal information is not collected for behavioral advertising on that inventory.

learn_more.source_title: Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: A Six-Step Compliance Plan for Your Business
Children's Online Privacy Protection Rule: Not Just for Kids' Sites
OpenRTB 2.6 specification, Regs object and the coppa flag

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed configuration documentation for setting child-directed treatment on inventory is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review which pages, sections, and apps are directed to children, then check whether the APS inventory serving on them is configured for child-directed treatment so personalized ads and tracking are suppressed.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the APS console and list the sites, apps, and ad slots configured for header bidding.
For each slice of inventory, check whether a child-directed or COPPA treatment is configured and record the setting found.
Cross-reference that list against the pages, sections, and apps the publisher considers directed to children, and note any child-directed inventory with no child-directed treatment set.
Ask the publisher who maintains the list of child-directed inventory and when it was last reviewed, and record the answer.

page_inspection.label: Page and request inspection
steps: Load a page or app screen that is directed to children and open the browser developer tools network panel.
Inspect the APS bid request and confirm whether the regulatory signal marks the request as child-directed, for example the OpenRTB coppa flag set to 1.
Check the responses for personalized demand, identity syncs, or tracking pixels, which indicate that child-directed suppression is not being applied.
Record, per inspected page, whether the child-directed signal is present and whether suppression appears to be taking effect.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each slice of inventory directed to children, whether a child-directed treatment is configured in APS and whether the bid request carries the child-directed signal. Inventory directed to children that is flagged so personalized ads and tracking are suppressed passes. Inventory directed to children that carries no child-directed treatment, or that shows personalized demand and tracking on inspection, is a fail recorded with the specific pages, sections, or apps affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which inventory is directed to children and whether each slice carries a child-directed treatment above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} serves inventory directed to children through Amazon Publisher Services (APS) without flagging it for child-directed treatment, so the bid requests carry no signal to suppress personalized advertising on those impressions. Under the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA), personal information from a child should not be collected for behavioral advertising, and the publisher is the party that knows which inventory is directed to children and so must set the signal. {{voice_recommend}} listing every page, section, and app that is directed to children, then configuring child-directed treatment on the APS inventory serving there so personalized ads, identity syncs, and tracking are suppressed. After the change, inspect a sample of those requests to confirm the child-directed signal is present and the suppression is taking effect.

audit_checks.title: Restricted data processing does not reach inventory likely to reach minors
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Inventory that is likely to reach minors, including teens between 13 and 16 covered by state privacy laws rather than COPPA, needs restricted data processing so personal information is not sold or used for targeted advertising. A gap between where minors actually are and where restricted processing is applied leaves that personal information available for uses the publisher did not intend, which is a compliance exposure short of the direct COPPA breach but still material.

educate.base:
Child-directed treatment under the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA) covers children under 13, but it is not the only data-protection obligation that touches young audiences. State privacy laws extend protections to minors, including teens between 13 and 16, who fall outside COPPA but are still owed limited data treatment. The control that carries this in the auction is restricted data processing, a mode in which personal information is not sold and is not used for cross-context behavioral or targeted advertising. The question for a publisher running Amazon Publisher Services (APS) is whether restricted data processing reaches the inventory that is likely to be seen by minors, not only the inventory that is strictly child-directed.

The signals that drive this in a programmatic auction are increasingly carried by the IAB Tech Lab Global Privacy Platform (GPP), which transmits privacy and consent choices, including the minor and age signals defined by US state privacy strings, from the page to demand partners. When a consent management platform sets those signals correctly and APS passes them, demand partners can apply restricted processing to impressions involving minors. When the signals are absent on inventory that reaches minors, the impression looks like any other and personal information can be used for targeted advertising.

The gap usually appears at the edges rather than in the obvious places. A publisher may flag a kids' section for COPPA correctly while leaving a teen-oriented area, a section about schools, or a young-adult vertical with no minor treatment at all, even though those areas draw an audience that state law protects. The audit looks for the inventory likely to reach minors that sits outside both the COPPA flagging and any restricted-processing configuration.

A few signs point to a restricted-data-processing gap:
  • Inventory aimed at teens or young audiences carries no minor signal and no restricted data processing, even though it sits outside the strictly child-directed sections that are flagged for COPPA
  • The consent management platform does not set or APS does not pass the GPP minor or age signals on inventory that reaches minors
  • The publisher treats COPPA flagging as the only youth-data obligation and has not mapped where teens and other minors fall under state privacy laws
You may want to confirm that restricted data processing covers the inventory likely to reach minors, including teen audiences that state law protects rather than COPPA. One approach is to map the audience of each section against the age thresholds in the state privacy laws that apply, then confirm the minor signals and restricted processing reach that inventory through APS.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a teen vertical, a section covering schools or youth sports, or entertainment content aimed at a young audience can draw minors who fall under state privacy laws rather than COPPA. You may want to confirm that restricted data processing reaches those sections through APS, so personal information from teens is not sold or used for targeted advertising even where COPPA does not apply.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, areas covering products for teens, school supplies, or young-adult interests can reach minors that state privacy laws protect. You may want to confirm that the minor signals and restricted data processing reach the APS inventory on those areas, so the personal information collected there is held to the limited treatment those audiences are owed.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's documentation for passing privacy signals and applying restricted data processing is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review where minors are likely to be reached across the inventory, then check whether the minor signals and restricted data processing reach that inventory through APS and the consent management platform.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the APS console and list the sites, apps, and ad slots in header bidding, noting which carry any minor or restricted-processing treatment.
Compare that list against the sections the publisher considers likely to reach minors, including teen and young-adult audiences, not only the strictly child-directed sections.
Record each slice of inventory likely to reach minors that carries no minor signal and no restricted data processing.

cmp_and_signal_inspection.label: Consent platform and signal inspection
steps: Load a page in a section likely to reach minors and inspect the consent management platform output and the GPP string it sets.
Confirm whether the minor or age signals defined by the applicable US state privacy strings are present, and whether the APS bid request carries them through to demand partners.
Check whether demand responses show targeted advertising or data uses that restricted processing should suppress, and record what was observed per section.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each slice of inventory likely to reach minors, whether the minor signals are set and passed through APS and whether restricted data processing applies. Inventory likely to reach minors where the minor signals reach demand partners and restricted processing is applied passes. Inventory likely to reach minors that carries no minor signal and no restricted processing is a fail recorded with the sections affected and the age threshold that applies.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which inventory is likely to reach minors and whether restricted data processing reaches each slice above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies restricted data processing only to its strictly child-directed inventory, leaving sections likely to reach minors, including teen audiences that state privacy laws protect rather than the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA), without minor signals or restricted processing through Amazon Publisher Services (APS). Where those signals are absent, personal information from minors can be sold or used for targeted advertising. {{voice_recommend}} mapping the audience of each section against the age thresholds in the state privacy laws that apply, then confirming the consent management platform sets the minor signals and APS passes them so restricted data processing reaches that inventory. After the change, inspect the signals on a sample of those requests to confirm the minor treatment is reaching demand partners.
